comments.text: One find the values of $\sigma(n)$ in https://oeis.org/A000203/b000203.txt. (there are 31525 many different values here, and the maximum multiplicity is 183, i.e. there is no $n$ for which there are more than $183$ different values $a$ with $\sigma(a)=n$) Related to the accompanying text below the question: "and claimed a similar proof for this problem." It is not for the problem, but it is a similar proof for the lim version instead of limsup version (https://www.renyi.hu/~p_erdos/1974-27.pdf, page 202)

[PoPo16] closely ties this problem to that of locating many primes which are one less than a smooth number (one uses products of such primes to create lots of collisions $\sigma(a)=\sigma(b)$). In particular, if for any fixed $\theta>1$ and large $y$ it can be shown that there are $\gg y^{\theta-o(1)}$ primes $p \leq y^\theta$ with $p+1$ $y$-rough, then the arguments in that paper give a positive answer to this question. (This type of result is known for $\theta \approx 3.377$, giving rise to a lower bound roughly of the form $h(x) \gg x^{1.4}$.)
